---
title: "ENVS 193DS Homework 3"
date: "May 31, 2026" 
author: "Danielle Sur" 
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
---

# Set up

```{r}
#| message: false

# loaded in packages
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(here)
library(readxl)

# read in data and saved as object called `kelp`
kelp <- read_csv("data/temp-kelp.csv")
# read in my data and saved as object called 'rxntime'
rxntime <- read_csv("data/rxn-time.csv")
```


# Problem 1. Giant kelp fronds

## a. An appropriate test

Pearson's R and Pearson's product-moment correlation tests 

ols

## b. Create a visualization

S

## c. Check your assumptions and run your test

a

## d. Results communication

## e. Test implications

## f. Double check your own work

# Problem 2. Personal data 

## a. Updating your visualizations

### Cleaning data

```{r}
# created a new object from rxntime
rxntime_type <- rxntime |> 
  # cleaned column names
 clean_names() |> 
  # selected columns of interest
  select(exercise_type, reaction_time_avg_ms) |> 
  # removed missing values
  drop_na(exercise_type, reaction_time_avg_ms) |> 
  # capitalized existing exercise type categories
  mutate(exercise_type = case_when(
    exercise_type == "biking" ~ "Biking",
    exercise_type == "volleyball" ~ "Volleyball",
    exercise_type == "walking" ~ "Walking"
  ))
```

### Figure 1.

```{r}
# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = rxntime_type,
       # x-axis: exercise type
       # y-axis: average reaction time in milliseconds
       # color by exercise type
       mapping = aes(x = "Exercise Type",
                     y = "Average Reaction Time (ms)",
                     color = exercise_type)) +
  # first layer: data points
  geom_point(data = rxntime_type,
             aes(x = exercise_type,
                 y = reaction_time_avg_ms,
                 size = 3)) +
  # changed theme
  theme_minimal() +
  # removed legend
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.background = element_blank(), # removing panel background
        panel.grid = element_blank(), # removing panel grid
        axis.ticks = element_blank() # removing axis ticks)
  ) +
  # relabeled x- and y- axes, adding title
  labs(title = "Exercise Type and Reaction Time",
       subtitle = "May 29, 2026",
       x = "Exercise Type",
       y = "Average Reaction Time (ms)") +
  # custom colors
  scale_color_manual(values =
                       c("Biking" = "magenta",
                         "Volleyball" = "purple3",
                         "Walking" = "blue"))
```

### Figure 2.

```{r}
# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = rxntime_clean,
       # x-axis: exercise duration in minutes
       # y-axis: average reaction time in milliseconds
       # color
       mapping = aes(x = "Exercise Type",
                     y = "Average Reaction Time (ms)")) +
  # first layer: data points
  geom_point(data = rxntime_clean,
             aes(x = exercise_duration_min,
                 y = reaction_time_avg_ms,
                 size = 3,
                 color = "hotpink")) +
  # changed theme
  theme_minimal() +
  # removed legend
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.background = element_blank(), # removing panel background
        panel.grid = element_blank(), # removing panel grid
        axis.ticks = element_blank(), # removing axis ticks
    ) +
  # relabeled x- and y- axes, adding title
  labs(title = "Exercise Duration and Reaction Time",
       subtitle = "May 29, 2026",
       x = "Exercise Duration (min)",
       y = "Average Reaction Time (ms)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = "hotpink")
```

## b. Captions

**Figure 1. Mean reaction times across biking, volleyball, and walking exercises.**
Points represent observations of mean reaction time (ms) after different kinds of exercise (pink: Biking, purple: volleyball, blue: walking). Data from my personal observations. Data accessed 2026-05-29. 

**Figure 2. Mean reaction times and exercise duration.**
Points represent observations of mean reaction time (ms) in relation to exercise duration (min). Data from my personal observations. Data accessed 2026-05-29.

# Problem 3. Affective visualization

## a. Describe in words what an affective visualization could look like for your personal data.

Taking inspiration from the Dear Data project, I could make a visual where each observation is represented by an icon (a stick figure for walking, a volleyball, and a bike tire for biking). Additional doodles such as a water droplet or a star can indicate whether water was consumed throughout exercise, or if an exercise exceeded a certain amount of time. Colors can be used to indicate levels of social interaction.

## b. Create a sketch (on paper) of your idea.

```{r fig-screenshot, echo=FALSE, out.width='400px'}
knitr::include_graphics("sketch.png")
```

## c. Make a draft of your visualization.

```{r fig-screenshot, echo=FALSE, out.width='400px'}
knitr::include_graphics("draft.png")
```

## d. Write an artist statement.

This is a visualization of the exercises I have done throughout the spring quarter. The icons arranged in an array of thirty observations each represent a type of exercise (walking, biking, or volleyball) while indicating factors such as socialization with other people and hydration. I was inspired most by the Dear Data project; especially by how colors and shapes were used not only to embellish the visualization but to tell a story. I created this draft with a sheet of A4 paper, a pen, and a couple highlighters. It is hand-drawn.

## e. Prep your materials to share in class.

View presentation slides [here](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1JCIGFIhqZ6NhE6zjIz3g13vsWye4e50MgZmk1uO-mqw/edit?usp=sharing).

# Problem 4. Statistical critique

## a. Revisit and summarize

## b. Visual clarity

## c. Aesthetic clarity

## d. Recommendations
